title: Action Verbs & Postpositions
learningGoal: Learn basic action verbs, command forms based on formality, and postpositions (in, on, from).
instructions: In this final block of Month 1, we will move beyond describing things and start talking about actions. You will learn how to tell people what to do, and you will learn Hindi's version of prepositions.

lessonContent.bodyText:
In Hindi, all verbs in their dictionary form end in 'na' (ना). This is called the infinitive form (meaning 'to do' something).

- खाना (Khaana) : To eat / Food
- पीना (Peena) : To drink
- जाना (Jaana) : To go
- आना (Aana) : To come
- करना (Karna) : To do

If you drop the 'na', you get the verb root: Kha, Pee, Jaa, Aa, Kar. We use the verb root to build everything else.

targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: To eat, to drink, to go, to come

lessonContent.bodyText:
To tell someone to do something, we attach a suffix to the verb root. The suffix changes based on how much respect you are showing.

Formal (Aap): Add 'iye' (इए) to the root.
- Jaa + iye = जाइये (Jaaiye) : Please go.
- Kha + iye = खाइये (Khaaiye) : Please eat.

Informal (Tum): Add 'o' (ओ) to the root.
- Jaa + o = जाओ (Jaao) : Go.
- Kha + o = खाओ (Khaao) : Eat.

Intimate/Rude (Tu): Just use the verb root.
- जा (Jaa) : Go!
- खा (Kha) : Eat!

The verb 'Karna' (to do) is slightly irregular when giving formal commands. 

Instead of Kar + iye = Kariye, it becomes कीजिये (Kijiye).
So if you want to say 'Please do this', say 'Yeh kijiye'.

For the informal 'Tum' form, it follows the rules: Kar + o = करो (Karo).

English uses pre-positions (they come BEFORE the noun: 'in the house'). Hindi uses post-positions (they come AFTER the noun: 'house in').

The big three postpositions are:
- में (mein) : In / Into
- पर (par) : On / At
- से (se) : From

Example: 'In the house' = घर में (Ghar mein).
Example: 'On the table' = मेज़ पर (Mez par).
Example: 'From America' = अमेरिका से (Amerika se).

targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: The book is on the table.

lessonContent.bodyText:
Let's put it all together. Remember the Hindi word order: Subject + Object/Location + Verb.

English: Go into the house.
Hindi: House into go. -> घर में जाओ। (Ghar mein jaao.)

English: Please come from America.
Hindi: America from please-come. -> अमेरिका से आइये। (Amerika se aaiye.)

learningObjective: The learner will accurately extract verb roots, form commands across formality levels, and place postpositions correctly after nouns.

examples.explanation: Pee (drink) + iye (formal request). Paani is the object.

examples.baseTranslation: The book is on the table.
examples.explanation: Mez (table) followed by par (on). Then the verb 'hai'.

importantRules.ruleText: Always use 'iye' for formal requests (Aap) and 'o' for informal requests (Tum). Mixing them up is grammatically incorrect and socially awkward.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Karna (to do) and Peena (to drink) are slightly irregular. Peena becomes Pijiye, and Karna becomes Kijiye.

importantRules.ruleText: Postpositions MUST come immediately after the noun they modify.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Unlike English 'in the big house', Hindi says 'big house in'.

cultureContext.contextText: Using the 'iye' form is the safest and most polite way to ask for anything in India. Even if you only know one word, like 'Pani' (water), saying 'Pani dijiye' (Please give water) will get you far.
dosList: Always default to the 'iye' verb ending when speaking to strangers or elders.
dontsList: Avoid using the root verb (Jaa, Kha) as a command unless you are very angry or talking to a close friend.

summary: You can now tell people what to do! You learned how to turn infinitives into polite and casual commands. You also learned the crucial postpositions 'mein', 'par', and 'se' which locate objects in space.

checkpointQuestions.questionText: What is the verb root of 'Jaana' (to go)?
How do you form a formal command for 'Khaana' (to eat)?
If 'Mez' means table, how do you say 'On the table'?

keyTakeaways: Drop 'na' to get the verb root.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.conceptSummary: Introduction to action verbs, imperatives, and postpositions.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.expectedUnderstanding: Learners can construct polite requests and locate objects using postpositions.

feedbackRules.onPass: Astounding! You have mastered Month 1 grammar.
feedbackRules.onFail: Review Section 2 carefully. The difference between 'iye' and 'o' is very important.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good effort! Remember that 'mein' means 'in' and 'se' means 'from'.

audioAssets.audioTranscript: जाना जाइये जाओ मेज़ पर घर में मैं घर में हूँ। पानी पीजिये।

hintText: Pay attention to the English translation. Notice how 'in the house' flips to 'house in'.
